1.synthesis: Paul comes to Corinth and finds Aquila and Priscilla, Jewish tentmakers recently expelled from Rome under Claudius's edict; he stays and works with them (Acts 18:1–3). He reasons in the synagogue every Sabbath; when Silas and Timothy arrive from Macedonia, he is occupied with the word, testifying that Jesus is the Christ (Acts 18:4–5). When the Jews oppose and revile him, Paul shakes out his garments: 'Your blood be on your own heads; I am innocent. From now on I will go to the Gentiles' (Acts 18:6). He moves next door to Crispus the synagogue ruler, who believes with his household (Acts 18:7–8). The Lord speaks in a night vision: 'Do not be afraid; speak and do not be silent, for I am with you' (Acts 18:9–10). Paul stays eighteen months. Gallio the proconsul dismisses the Jewish charges against him (Acts 18:12–17).
1.historical_context: Corinth was the capital of the Roman province of Achaia—a major commercial city at the isthmus connecting mainland Greece with the Peloponnese. The expulsion of Jews from Rome by Claudius (c. 49 AD, mentioned by Suetonius as relating to disturbances over 'Chrestus') brought Aquila and Priscilla to Corinth; they are key figures in the Pauline mission and later mentioned in Romans 16:3 and 1 Corinthians 16:19. The Gallio inscription at Delphi is the most precise chronological anchor in Acts, dating Paul's Corinth stay to c. 51–52 AD. Crispus appears in 1 Corinthians 1:14 as one of the few Paul baptized personally. The eighteen months at Corinth is Paul's longest stay in any city in Acts.
1.literary_structure: The Corinth narrative is structured around three divine reassurances: the Lord's night vision (vv.9–10), the legal protection through Gallio (vv.12–17), and the extended fruitful stay. The vision comes at what Paul's letters suggest was a moment of personal weakness (cf. 1 Corinthians 2:3: 'I was with you in weakness and in fear and in much trembling'). The Gallio episode provides the legal precedent that Judaism is a 'licit religion' within which Christianity operates—Gallio's refusal to adjudicate internal Jewish disputes gives the nascent church legal breathing room in the Roman world.
1.theological_application: 'Do not be afraid; speak and do not be silent, for I am with you' (Acts 18:9–10) is the gospel's counter to the fear that silences witnesses. The Lord's vision does not remove the threat but promises accompaniment through it—'I am with you' is the divine presence that transforms dangerous situations from paralyzing to navigable. The church's ministry of proclamation is always vulnerable to the fear of opposition, rejection, and legal threat; the Lord's promise of presence is the foundation of prophetic courage. Paul's eighteen months in Corinth—his longest stay—is the fruit of the Lord's reassurance: obedience produces extended fruitful ministry.
1.christology: 'There are many in this city who are my people' (Acts 18:10) is the risen Christ's sovereign knowledge of his elect in Corinth before they have come to faith—the same predestinarian confidence that grounded Acts 13:48 ('as many as were appointed to eternal life believed'). The risen Lord has a people in Corinth whom Paul's proclamation will reach; the mission is not at risk of total failure because the outcome is in the Lord's hands. This is the Corinthian mission's theological foundation: not Paul's skill or the city's receptivity, but the Lord's prior election and accompanying presence.

18.synthesis: Paul stays many days longer, then sets sail for Syria with Priscilla and Aquila; at Cenchreae he cuts his hair, for he was under a vow (Acts 18:18). They arrive at Ephesus; Paul reasons with the Jews in the synagogue but declines when they ask him to stay longer, saying 'I will return if God wills' (Acts 18:19–21). He lands at Caesarea, goes up to greet the church, then comes down to Antioch (Acts 18:22). After spending some time there, he departs, passing through Galatia and Phrygia, strengthening all the disciples (Acts 18:23).
18.historical_context: The vow at Cenchreae (Corinth's eastern port, v.18) is likely a Nazirite vow (Numbers 6:1–21), indicating Paul's continued practice of Jewish religious disciplines even as apostle to the Gentiles. The compressed return narrative covers substantial geography: Ephesus (a brief preview), Caesarea, Jerusalem (implied in 'the church'), and Antioch—summarizing the end of the Second Missionary Journey in a few verses. 'If God wills' (v.21) is Paul's characteristic deference to divine providence in travel planning, consistent with his letters (Romans 1:10; 1 Corinthians 4:19). The strengthening tour through Galatia and Phrygia opens the Third Missionary Journey.
18.literary_structure: This transitional section bridges the Second and Third Missionary Journeys with remarkable compression. The Ephesus preview (vv.19–21) plants the seed for the extended Ephesian ministry of chapter 19—Paul departs promising return, leaving Priscilla and Aquila behind as advance workers. The Antioch report (v.22) repeats the journey-end pattern of the First Journey: return to Antioch, report, rest, then depart again strengthening the established churches. The vow and the deferred Ephesus visit show a Paul who is strategically deliberate, not impulsive, in his mission movements.
18.theological_application: Paul's Nazirite vow is a reminder that the apostle to the Gentiles remained rooted in Jewish religious practice throughout his ministry—his principle of being 'all things to all people' extends to continued observance of Jewish disciplines in Jewish contexts. The church's theology of freedom from the law as a condition of salvation does not require abandonment of all Jewish practice for Jewish believers; Paul's own example shows a more nuanced pastoral posture. 'If God wills' (v.21) also models the Christian disposition to all future planning: holding intentions lightly, acknowledging divine sovereignty over outcomes.
18.christology: Paul's return circuit—Ephesus, Caesarea, Jerusalem, Antioch—traces the geography of the whole gospel story: the church of the Gentile mission (Antioch), the apostolic center (Jerusalem), the threshold of the Ephesian mission (Caesarea). The risen Christ who sends his servants out also calls them back for renewal and regrouping before the next advance. The strengthening of 'all the disciples' (Acts 18:23) in Galatia and Phrygia is the risen Lord's pastoral care for the communities planted in his name, exercised through the returning apostle.

24.synthesis: A Jew named Apollos, a native of Alexandria, comes to Ephesus—eloquent, competent in the scriptures, instructed in the way of the Lord, fervent in spirit, speaking and teaching accurately the things concerning Jesus, though knowing only the baptism of John (Acts 18:24–25). Priscilla and Aquila hear him in the synagogue and take him aside, explaining to him the way of God more accurately (Acts 18:26). He wishes to cross to Achaia; the brothers encourage him and write to the disciples there to welcome him. He greatly helps those who through grace had believed, confuting the Jews publicly and powerfully showing from the scriptures that Jesus was the Christ (Acts 18:27–28).
24.historical_context: Alexandria was the center of the Jewish diaspora's most sophisticated intellectual culture—Philo's allegorical biblical interpretation flourished there. Apollos's description ('eloquent, competent in the scriptures') reflects this Alexandrian formation. He knew Jesus and had received John's baptism but not Spirit-baptism, suggesting he had encountered the pre-Pentecost gospel tradition without the full post-resurrection development. Priscilla and Aquila's quiet private instruction—rather than public correction—is a model of gracious theological formation. Apollos subsequently becomes a significant figure in Corinth (1 Corinthians 1:12; 1 Corinthians 3:4–6), where Paul addresses factionalism around his preaching.
24.literary_structure: The Apollos episode continues the education theme: just as the Bereans tested Paul's teaching against scripture, Aquila and Priscilla privately complete Apollos's formation. The pattern is generous—they do not silence or displace him but deepen him. The Ephesian community's letter of commendation for Apollos (v.27) demonstrates the inter-community network that Paul's mission has created: churches endorsing traveling teachers to one another. Apollos's Corinthian ministry—'greatly helped those who believed' and 'confuted the Jews'—shows the fruit of completed theological formation operating through natural gift.
24.theological_application: The Apollos episode models how the church handles theologically incomplete but genuinely gifted teachers: not public rebuke but private, careful, gracious completion of what is lacking. Priscilla and Aquila's approach—'they took him aside and explained to him the way of God more accurately'—is the model of theological mentoring that preserves relationship and dignity while ensuring doctrinal completeness. The church in every generation has gifted communicators who need theological deepening, and the community's health depends on those with deeper understanding being willing to invest privately in those who need completion—not to silence gifts but to complete them.
24.christology: Apollos's incomplete knowledge—accurate about Jesus but knowing only John's baptism—points to the decisive christological development between John's anticipatory message and the post-resurrection gospel: the Spirit poured out by the risen and exalted Christ. What Apollos lacked was not information about Jesus' life and teaching but the full theological weight of the resurrection, ascension, and Spirit-gift. After Priscilla and Aquila's instruction, he can show 'from the scriptures that Jesus was the Christ' (v.28) with comprehensive force—the whole canonical witness to the Messiah now operating through his Alexandrian gift.
